questionText: A curve is given parametrically by $x = t^{2}$ and $y = t + 1$, where $t \ge 0$. The line $y = x - 5$ crosses the curve. Find the coordinates of the point of intersection.

steps.description: Note the curve and the line
steps.workingLatex: \text{Curve: } x = t^{2},\ y = t + 1 \qquad \text{Line: } y = x - 5
steps.explanation: To find where they meet we force the curve's coordinates to satisfy the line, so we will substitute the parametric $x$ and $y$ into the line equation.
At any intersection the coordinates fit the line, so replacing $x$ with $t^{2}$ and $y$ with $t + 1$ gives one equation in $t$.
Bringing everything to one side produces a tidy quadratic whose roots are the candidate parameter values.
Two numbers multiplying to $-6$ and adding to $-1$ are $-3$ and $+2$, giving the factors.
Each bracket set to zero gives a possible value of the parameter.
The curve is only defined for $t \ge 0$, so $t = -2$ does not correspond to any point on this curve and must be discarded. Only $t = 3$ survives.

steps.description: See why the discarded root is not on the curve
steps.explanation: Although $(4,\,-1)$ does satisfy the line, the parameter $-2$ lies outside $t \ge 0$, so this point is simply not part of the curve we were given.

steps.description: Find the coordinates at the valid value
steps.explanation: Substituting the allowed value $t = 3$ into the parametric equations gives the genuine intersection point.

steps.description: Verify the point lies on the line
steps.explanation: Checking the point against $y = x - 5$ confirms it truly lies on both the curve and the line.
This is the only intersection consistent with the allowed range of $t$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The curve meets the line at $(9,\,4)$, when $t = 3$. The other root $t = -2$ is rejected because it lies outside the range $t \ge 0$.
